content.text: A GenAI developer platform team that operates without an explicit roadmap defaults to "fix what's loudest" — and loudest usually means whatever the most vocal team escalated last week. A platform product roadmap imposes structure on that chaos by linking developer pain signals to strategic objectives, capacity to commitments, and commitments to transparent communication. Unlike a feature-team roadmap, a platform roadmap must serve many internal customers simultaneously, balance reliability investment against new capabilities, and maintain developer trust through predictability.

Platform teams serve internal customers who cannot switch to a competitor. This creates a specific dynamic: developers tolerate friction they cannot avoid, but they lose trust in the platform team when roadmap promises are broken or improvements never ship. Roadmapping for a platform must solve three problems that feature-team roadmapping does not face:

Team A wants eval runner improvements; team B wants prompt registry versioning; team C wants Slack notification support. A feature team has a product manager who represents one user persona. A platform team must represent dozens of teams with different maturity levels, different tech stacks, and different pain points.
Reliability investment competes with new capabilities.

Fixing a flaky SDK retry is less visible than launching a new evaluation framework, but it affects every team every day. A roadmap without an explicit reliability budget will systematically under-invest in the work that keeps the existing user base happy.

Internal customers accept that plans change. They do not accept promises that silently disappear. A public roadmap that is updated when plans change — even to say "this slipped" — builds more trust than a private roadmap that always looks clean.

Every backlog item should trace to a strategic objective. The hierarchy has four levels: OKRs at the quarter level, epics at the multi-week level, stories at the sprint level, and tasks at the day level. The connection is explicit — each epic references the OKR it serves.

# Example: one quarterly OKR with two epics
okr:
  quarter: Q3-2026
  objective: "Accelerate GenAI first-deploy for all new teams"
  key_results:
    - id: KR1
      metric: "Time-to-first-deploy P75"
      target: "≤ 3 days"
      baseline: "6.2 days (Q2-2026)"
    - id: KR2
      metric: "DevEx survey score (onboarding dimension)"
      target: "≥ 4.0 / 5.0"
      baseline: "3.4 (Q2-2026)"

epics:
  - id: E-2026Q3-01
    title: "Scaffold template: zero-config LLM quickstart"
    okr_refs: [KR1]
    effort_weeks: 3
    owner: "@alice"
    description: >
      Add a Backstage software template that generates a complete
      project with LLM SDK, eval test suite, and GitHub Actions
      pipeline. A developer with no prior platform experience
      should reach their first LLM API call within 30 minutes.
    success_criteria:
      - "M1 milestone (SDK install) drops from 2.1 days to < 4 hours at P75"
      - "Template used by ≥ 5 new teams in Q3"

  - id: E-2026Q3-02
    title: "Self-serve API key provisioning via Backstage"
    okr_refs: [KR1, KR2]
    effort_weeks: 4
    owner: "@bob"
    description: >
      Replace the ticket-based API key request process with a
      Backstage template that provisions keys automatically via
      Vault and applies quota policies from the team's tier.
    success_criteria:
      - "M2 milestone (API key obtained) drops from 3.4 days to < 1 day at P75"
      - "Zero manual provisioning tickets in Q3"

A platform backlog has four distinct categories of work, each with its own budget. Without explicit budget allocation, new-capability work consistently crowds out reliability and tech-debt investment — because new features have vocal champions and reliability work does not.
Net-new features: new tools, new integrations, new APIs
Platform growth; closes adoption gaps; addresses strategic OKRs
P1/P2 friction fixes, SLO breach remediation, error rate reduction
Keeps existing users productive; builds trust; prevents churn to workarounds
Internal quality improvements, dependency upgrades, architecture evolution
Maintains velocity; prevents the codebase from becoming a barrier to new capabilities
Mandatory security patches, compliance controls, vulnerability remediation
Non-negotiable; emergencies displace other work, so reserve capacity avoids plan collapse

The specific percentages matter less than making the allocation explicit and defending it. When the team commits to "20% reliability budget", it becomes a tool for declining scope creep: "we can add that to the backlog, but it would push out our reliability commitments." Without explicit allocation, every trade-off is re-litigated in every planning meeting.
A platform roadmap without structured stakeholder input produces a team that is optimising for the wrong things. Three mechanisms feed developer priorities into the planning process:

A rotating group of 5–8 engineers from different product teams who meet quarterly with the platform team. Members are nominated by their engineering managers and serve for two quarters. The advisory group:
Reviews the draft roadmap for the upcoming quarter and raises concerns before commitments are made public.
Surfaces cross-team friction that the platform team may not have observed in quantitative data.
Validates that roadmap items address the actual developer experience, not a platform-team assumption of it.
Acts as an early-adopter cohort for new features before general release.
Significant changes to platform APIs, defaults, or behaviour require a written RFC (Request for Comments). The RFC template is versioned in the platform repository and follows a standard structure:

# RFC-NNNN: [Title]

**Author:** @handle
**Status:** Draft | Open for Comment | Accepted | Rejected | Superseded
**Created:** YYYY-MM-DD
**Decision by:** YYYY-MM-DD

## Summary
One paragraph: what are you proposing and why?

## Motivation
What problem does this solve? Link to friction items, survey data,
or adoption funnel metrics that justify the change.

## Detailed Design
The technical specification. Include API surface changes, migration
paths for existing users, and backwards-compatibility implications.

## Alternatives Considered
What else was evaluated? Why was this approach chosen?

## Drawbacks
Honest assessment of costs, risks, and what this approach gives up.

## Open Questions
Unresolved decisions that need input during the comment period.

## Decision Record
(Filled in by TC when the comment period closes.)

RFCs are open for comment for a minimum of 10 business days. Any engineer can comment. The TC Council reviews all comments and documents the decision with reasoning. Rejected RFCs are not deleted — they remain as a record of alternatives considered.

The triaged friction backlog (see Platform Friction Tracking) is the primary quantitative demand signal for reliability and quick-wins work. P1 and P2 items automatically enter the next sprint planning conversation. The friction backlog score — weighted by signal count, affected teams, and severity — is used to compare items when capacity is constrained.
Planning happens in five sequential steps in the two weeks before the quarter starts:
Platform PM aggregates: friction backlog, survey themes, advisory group requests, mandatory security items, and OKR draft from engineering leadership. All inputs are normalised into candidate epics.

Platform TC Council counts available engineering-weeks for the quarter, subtracts planned leave, and applies category budgets. Result: a capacity envelope by category (e.g., 12 weeks for new capabilities, 6 for reliability, 4 for tech debt, 2 reserved).

Epics are scored on impact × reach / effort and sorted. TCs fill the capacity envelope from the top of each category. Epics that don't fit are pushed to the following quarter's candidate list, not silently dropped.
Draft roadmap is shared with the advisory group for 48 hours of async feedback. Material concerns trigger a synchronous 30-minute call to resolve; minor feedback is incorporated as annotations.
Final roadmap is published to the internal TechDocs page, the Backstage software catalog, and announced in the monthly digest and Slack channel.
The public-facing roadmap uses a Now / Next / Later format rather than fixed calendar dates. This communicates intent without over-committing to specific dates that slip when reality intervenes.

# GenAI Platform Roadmap — Q3 2026

## Now (in active development this sprint)
- [E-2026Q3-01] Scaffold template: zero-config LLM quickstart (owner: @alice, ETA: Aug 15)
- [E-2026Q3-02] Self-serve API key provisioning (owner: @bob, ETA: Aug 31)
- [friction-20260601-001] Fix SDK retry logic to respect Retry-After header (owner: @carol)

## Next (planned for later this quarter)
- [E-2026Q3-03] Eval runner CI gate: configurable regression thresholds
- [E-2026Q3-04] Prompt registry: semantic versioning and auto-deprecation
- Quick-wins sprint — July (targeting 6–8 P3/P4 items)

## Later (candidate for Q4 2026 — not committed)
- LLM cost attribution dashboard (team-level spend breakdown)
- Multi-model eval: side-by-side comparison in eval runner UI
- Backstage plugin: real-time quota consumption widget
- Streaming support in LLM SDK (async generators)

## Recently shipped (Q2 2026)
- ✅ Backstage self-serve API key provisioning template
- ✅ Eval runner: parallel worker execution (3× throughput)
- ✅ Prompt registry: Jinja2 variable support
- ✅ 14 quick-win friction fixes

_Last updated: 2026-07-01 | Questions: #genai-platform-updates_

Plans change. An unexpected P1 security issue, a critical hire leaving, or a dependency delay can shift the roadmap. The key is to communicate changes promptly rather than hoping to catch up silently.
Update ETA in the public roadmap; note the reason
Within 2 business days of the decision
Update roadmap; add a note in the monthly digest explaining the slip
New P1 forces a scope change
Slack announcement in #genai-platform-updates; update roadmap same day; note what was deferred
Advisory group briefing first; then public announcement with 2-week notice before the new direction takes effect
2 weeks before the new quarter starts

When a commitment slips, the announcement should include: what slipped, the new expected date or quarter, and the reason. "We pushed eval runner CI gates to Q4 because the P1 gateway outage on July 5 took two weeks to remediate" is more trust-building than silence or a vague "reprioritisation".
A backlog that grows without pruning becomes a graveyard of stale items that erode confidence in the planning process. The following hygiene rules keep the backlog actionable:
P3 items with no sprint slot after 6 months are reviewed for wont-fix or reclassification. The review is documented on the item.
Duplicate epics are merged when detected. The surviving epic lists all source tickets so reporters can follow it.
"Nice-to-have" requests without a concrete champion (a specific team willing to provide feedback on the implementation) are moved to the Later section with the label needs-champion.
Tech debt items that are no longer relevant (because the code was rewritten or removed) are closed with the label no-longer-applicable.
The backlog is reviewed for staleness at the start of every quarterly planning cycle. Items older than 3 quarters with no action are either committed or closed.

Capacity sizing translates available engineering headcount into a concrete capacity envelope per category. Scoring epics consistently prevents planning meetings from becoming debates where the loudest voice wins.

The priority score formula is intentionally simple. Changing the formula mid-quarter creates confusion, so establish it at the start of the year and apply it consistently. The score is a tiebreaker and a starting point for conversation — it does not override mandatory security work or explicit OKR alignment.

Roadmap disputes arise when a team believes their friction is being underweighted or when a platform decision breaks their existing workflow. The decision-rights framework prevents these disputes from stalling delivery.
All teams using the API (via RFC process, minimum 10 days)
RFC open period + 5 days for TC vote
Slack notification to advisory group within 24h
None required for P4; advisory group notification for P1–P3

Escalation beyond the platform team goes to the engineering director. Escalations require the escalating team to document: the specific decision they are contesting, the impact to their team (with data), and what alternative decision they are requesting. Unsubstantiated escalations are declined at the platform lead level without escalation.
The roadmap is a means to an end. The platform team measures whether the roadmap process is producing the intended outcomes, not just whether epics are being shipped.
Epics shipped as committed / epics planned at quarter start
Time from P1 creation to fix in production
Days between a plan change and its appearance in the public roadmap
1–5 Likert score from advisory group members at quarter end
Quarterly survey composite score (onboarding, reliability, tooling)

A GenAI platform product roadmap is the mechanism that connects developer pain signals to delivered improvements in a transparent, predictable way. It works by maintaining a structured hierarchy from OKRs to epics to stories, explicitly budgeting capacity across work categories, pulling demand from multiple stakeholder input channels, and publishing commitments in a format that developers can hold the team accountable to. The quarterly planning cycle gives the team a regular cadence to reset priorities. The Now / Next / Later format communicates intent without over-committing to dates. And prompt, honest communication when plans change builds the trust that sustains long-term developer satisfaction with the platform.
